title: Project Abstract (PDF extracted to JSON)

pages.text:
Abstract: Modeling Diet-Driven Microbiome and Neurotransmitter Pathways 
Influencing Cognitive Performance 
Diet influences cognitive performance through complex biological pathways involving the gut 
microbiome and neurochemical signaling, yet most human studies remain correlational and lack 
mechanistic explanation. This project develops a multi-stage computational modeling framework 
to simulate how dietary patterns affect cognition through biologically intermediate layers of the 
gut–brain axis. Publicly available dietary, microbiome, and metabolomics datasets are integrated 
to model the sequence diet → gut microbiome composition → neurotransmitter-related 
metabolite production → cognitive performance. Supervised machine learning models are 
trained at each stage to predict microbial genera from nutrient intake, metabolite profiles from 
microbial composition, and cognitive outcomes from metabolite patterns. The integrated pipeline 
is then validated using a previously collected, de-identified dataset of high school students 
containing diet quality scores and cognitive performance measures. The model is expected to 
demonstrate that nutrient-dense dietary patterns, particularly higher fiber and polyunsaturated fat 
intake with lower added sugar, predict microbiome profiles enriched in short-chain fatty 
acid–producing and neuroactive taxa, leading to metabolite signatures associated with improved 
attention and memory. By explicitly modeling biological intermediates rather than relying on 
direct associations, this research advances mechanistic understanding of diet–cognition 
relationships and provides a scalable framework for predictive, biologically interpretable 
nutrition and brain health research.
